document_metadata.confidence_calibration: Uncalibrated - will adjust after 10+ pilot analyses

findings.title: Direct Contradiction in Milestone Compensation Amount
findings.explanation:
Section 3, Milestones & Deliverables Table: For the milestone 'Contracts executed — early adopter cohort', the compensation trigger is '3% of up-front revenue captured for early adopter cohort'.

vs

EXHIBIT A — COMPENSATION SCHEDULE Table: For the milestone 'Signed contracts w/ monetary payment', the 'Bonus Amount' is '$__________'.

Problem: There is a direct conflict between how a key milestone bonus is calculated. Section 3 defines it as a percentage of revenue, which is variable. Exhibit A defines it as a fixed (but currently blank) dollar amount. This creates total uncertainty about how the contractor will be paid for achieving a primary objective. It's an unresolved term that makes the agreement unenforceable as written regarding this payment.

findings.title: Ambiguous Ownership of Intellectual Property Derivatives
findings.explanation:
Section 9.1 Work Product: 'All deliverables, materials, and work product created by the Contractor specifically for [Company Name] in the performance of services under this SOW... shall be the sole property of [Company Name].'

vs

Section 9.2 LLM Framework — Contractor IP: 'Any enhancements, adaptations, or derivatives of the Contractor IP developed during this engagement shall be addressed in the Second SOW or a separate IP agreement...'

Problem: The contractor's core task (Section 2.1) is to create a 'strategic roadmap' and 'design and initiate use of the LLM framework as a pre-sales discovery and qualification tool'. It is ambiguous whether this 'roadmap' and 'design' constitute 'work product' owned by the company under 9.1, or 'enhancements, adaptations, or derivatives' of the contractor's IP with ownership deferred under 9.2. If it's considered 'work product', the company could own the implementation plan for the contractor's core IP, potentially cutting him out of a future deal while using his strategic plan.

findings.title: Contradictory Governing Bonus Schedules
findings.explanation:
Section 6.2 Milestone-Based Incentive Bonuses: '...the Contractor will be eligible for the following milestone-based incentive bonuses upon verified achievement – see table in section 3'.

vs

EXHIBIT A — COMPENSATION SCHEDULE: The document includes a separate, standalone compensation schedule in Exhibit A with slightly different milestone descriptions, a blank value, and additional terms (e.g., 'max').

Problem: The agreement explicitly directs the reader to Section 3 for the bonus schedule, but then provides a different, and conflicting, schedule in Exhibit A. This creates a legal inconsistency as to which part of the contract governs milestone payments. In a dispute, both parties could point to the schedule that best serves their interest.

findings.title: Inconsistent Terms for $1M Capital Raise Bonus
findings.explanation:
Section 3, Milestones & Deliverables Table: The compensation trigger for the $1M raise milestone is '3% of first $1M raised if achieved'.

vs

EXHIBIT A — COMPENSATION SCHEDULE Table: The 'Bonus Amount' for the same milestone is '$30,000 (max) (3% of $1M)'.

Problem: Section 3 implies a straightforward 3% calculation. Exhibit A introduces the qualifier '(max)', which creates ambiguity. Does '(max)' mean the bonus could be less than $30,000 under some circumstances? Or is it simply clarifying that 3% of $1M is the maximum payout? The addition of this single word creates an inconsistency that could be exploited in a dispute to reduce the payment.

findings.title: Unilateral and Subjective Success Metric for Key Milestone
findings.explanation:
Section 3, Milestones & Deliverables Table: The 'Success Metric' for the 'LLM assessment' milestone is 'LOI drafted by Company & expressing interest and intent to support development and market strategy in Q3'.

vs

Section 2.1 Goal: '...produce a targeted Q3 roadmap and initial test results that demonstrate whether and how the LLM assessment framework can be formally incorporated...'

Problem: The success of the LLM milestone is not based on the quality of the contractor's work (e.g., delivery of a roadmap), but on the company's subjective action of drafting an LOI. The company has sole discretion to decide whether it has 'interest and intent'. This gives the company unilateral control to declare the milestone a failure, regardless of the contractor's performance. This creates a significant imbalance of power.

findings.title: Ambiguity in 'Earned' Bonuses Upon Termination
findings.explanation:
Section 10 Termination: 'In the event of termination by [Company Name] without cause, the Contractor shall retain any milestone bonuses earned through the date of termination. Any milestone bonuses contingent on future events shall be forfeited...'

vs

EXHIBIT A — COMPENSATION SCHEDULE: Lists payment timing as 'Within 10 days of milestone verification' or 'Within 30 days of verified capital raise'.

Problem: A perverse incentive exists for the company. The contractor could achieve a milestone (e.g., getting contracts signed on May 27), but the resulting capital raise may not occur until June 8. If the company terminates the contractor on June 1st without cause, they could argue the $30,000 bonus for the capital raise is 'contingent on a future event' (the money arriving) and is therefore forfeited, even though the contractor's work directly led to that event.

findings.title: Conflicting Agreement Start and End Dates
findings.explanation:
ENGAGEMENT OVERVIEW: 'Effective Date: 3/30/26', 'Initial Term: Three (3) months — Trial Period ending 6/30/26'.

vs

Section 5.1 Trial Period: 'The initial term of this engagement ('Trial Period') commences on [START DATE] and runs for three (3) months, through [END DATE].'

Problem: The document contains both specific, defined dates and placeholder dates for the same term. This is a direct inconsistency that creates confusion about the official start and end of the engagement. While likely a clerical error, it makes the document look unprofessional and could be used to dispute deadlines if not corrected.
